does: This paper introduces the Uncertainty-aware Reward Model (URM) and its ensemble variant URME, which enhance reward modeling for LLM alignment by quantifying uncertainty in human preference prediction. URM uses a probabilistic value head to capture aleatoric uncertainty through modeling distributions of disentangled human preference attributes, while URME adds epistemic uncertainty quantification by measuring disagreement across an ensemble of individual URMs. The authors demonstrate that these models achieve strong performance on RewardBench compared to larger-scale baselines and show consistent improvements when integrated into downstream LLM training methods including best-of-n sampling, iterative DPO, and PPO, with experiments indicating that uncertainty-guided predictions lead to better generation quality.
connects: This work builds on the foundational literature on reward modeling for LLM alignment, extending beyond standard point-estimate reward functions that fail to capture the inherent stochasticity in human preferences. It connects to prior work on uncertainty quantification in machine learning, particularly ensemble methods for epistemic uncertainty and probabilistic modeling for aleatoric uncertainty, adapting these techniques to the reward modeling context. The paper responds to limitations in existing reward models that lack reliability assessment mechanisms, addressing a gap between RLHF frameworks and more recent preference optimization approaches like DPO that assume reliable preference signals. It also builds on the broader line of work examining how reward model quality impacts downstream LLM performance.
enables: By providing uncertainty estimates alongside reward predictions, this work enables more robust downstream applications including selective sampling strategies that avoid unreliable evaluations, principled exploration during LLM training, and better decision-making in human-in-the-loop systems. The disentanglement of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty opens pathways for targeted improvements, such as collecting more data for high-epistemic-uncertainty cases or handling genuinely ambiguous preferences better. Future work could leverage uncertainty signals for active learning of reward models, curriculum learning strategies that avoid problematic uncertain regions, and more sophisticated integration of uncertainty into policy optimization algorithms beyond the tested methods, potentially advancing toward reward models that gracefully handle distribution shift and genuine preference disagreement.
